notes: Only one for loop with a single if-len check; transformation is mechanical (strip+append+default arg). Meets Accept threshold (one D2 score).

Reject: Python normalizes 3 query input shapes (dict/list[str]/list[tuple]) via isinstance checks; Go output silently drops all normalization, only accepting pre-normalized []QueryPair. The instruction explicitly cites R11 (isinstance) but no isinstance handling appears in output. Both D2=1 (silent logic drop) and D4=1 (R11 cited with no visible instance in output) trigger reject.

Both functions are map-iteration expansions of set-to-slice and list-comprehension-to-loop; no statement-level branching inside the loops beyond the iteration itself. Meets Accept threshold (one D5=2).

Review: Near-duplicate of 413 — both translate the same fetch+get_field_names pair pattern (cache lookup with error on miss, then field alias normalization via explicit for loop into map[string]struct{}, then sort). DynamoDBCrawler omits the knownContainers loop but the transformation pattern is essentially identical to CosmosCrawler (413). D6=2 and D5=2 (no branching in fetch; get_field_names is a single loop with alias ok-check). Two D2 scores trigger Review.

Review: `rune(text[pos])` treats a byte as a rune; seps string contains U+2013 and U+2014 (multi-byte UTF-8), so the separator check will never match those characters when they appear in text. Fixable by using utf8.DecodeRuneInString. D1=2 (one fixable issue), D2=2 (behavioral drift for non-ASCII separators). Two D2 scores trigger Review.

structuralLevels parameter typed as [][2]interface{} with unguarded type assertions pair[0].(string) and pair[1].(int); will panic on wrong-type input. A typed struct would be idiomatic. D2=2 (non-trivial behavioral risk in non-critical path). Meets Accept threshold (one D2=2).

Review: Input field contains a Go statement `contents = append(contents, c.content)` instead of Python `contents.append(c.content)`. This is a Go artifact in the Python input field, making the input inconsistent (M5). D3=1 triggers Review.

Go output drops tmp_embeddings list build, emb_array construction, and tmp_store.add_document() call entirely. Only buildCommunity() is called. The embedding logic and document store population — core to the function — are silently omitted.

R04 (list comprehension -> for loop) is cited in both instruction and D4, but the input has no list comprehension — only a plain for-range loop. The texts=[c.content for c in chunks] comprehension belongs to _embed_chunks (ID 429), not this function. Minor: two rule-citation issues but transformation is otherwise correct.

Sub-block is extracted verbatim from ID 436's output (the agglomerative distance-matrix block at lines 751-878 of ID 436). Training on both the sub-block and the full function it was taken from teaches nothing new. D3=2 because the sub-block instruction has no standalone function boundary, making it ambiguous whether imports/declarations are expected.

Distance-matrix building block is near-identical to ID 436 (agglomerative). Full DBSCAN function adds distinct noise-point handling logic, so D6=2 rather than 1. Single minor uniqueness issue does not trigger Review.

Input is a 9-line code snippet (noise-point handling loop only). D5=1 because input < 10 lines with minimal standalone logic. D6=1 because this exact code block appears verbatim in ID 438's output (lines 994-1013). D3=2 because the sub-block instruction lacks function context. Two dimensions score 1 -> Reject.

Cohesion-computation block is extracted verbatim from ID 441's output (lines 1203-1232). Teaching the sub-block after the full method adds no new pattern. D3=2 because the sub-block input lacks struct receiver context making it ambiguous. D6=1 triggers Review.

Type assertion `assignments.(map[string]string)` will silently fail for JSON-decoded data (which yields map[string]interface{}), causing entityToCluster to remain empty. Minor behavioral drift in a non-critical deserialization path — one dim at 2, rest 3 -> Accept.

Non-compiling code: inside `if techID, ok := tech["id"].(string); ok { mits, exists := ... if exists { mitigations = mit }; _ = mit }` — variable `mit` is undefined (should be `mits`). Two references to undefined `mit` make this non-compilable Go. D1=1 -> Reject.

Cooccurrence pair key changed from tuple (a,b) to string a+"|"+b — pipe character in chunk IDs would corrupt key uniqueness. Minor behavioral drift in non-critical path; reviewer should confirm chunk ID alphabet excludes '|'.

D5=1: Python input is 15 lines of pure field assignments with zero statement-level branches or loops — constructor body is trivially mechanical. D6=2: Same constructor pattern (R01+R02+R12, all-field-assignment body) as ID 475 (NerPipeline.__init__) in this batch.

D6=2: Near-identical normalization pattern as ID 474 (EntityNormalizer.Normalize) in this batch — both from _normalizer.py, both strip symbols, split tokens, filter empty, singularize last token with same rule set (R04, R10, R11). One is a standalone function and one is a method, but the transformation pattern is essentially the same.

D6=2: Near-identical normalization pattern as ID 471 (normalize_entity) in this batch — both from _normalizer.py, both apply the same strip/split/filter/singularize pipeline with the same rules. One is a standalone function and one is a pointer-receiver method, but the core transformation is the same.

D5=1: Python input is 19 lines of pure field assignments with zero statement-level branches or loops — constructor body has no logic. D6=2: Same constructor pattern (R01+R02+R12, all-field-assignment body) as ID 459 (EntityGraphPipeline.__init__) in this batch.

Input has only 9 non-empty code lines (function body is a simple for loop with one if-check). Rules applied are mechanical (R05, R06, R10, R11) with no complex type conversion. Fails D5 < 10 lines threshold.

Input field is corrupted: Python source uses Go-style struct literal brace syntax with keyword args (`EntityMatch{entity_id=gm.entity_id, name=gm.name, ...}`) which is not valid Python (Python uses parentheses for dataclass/namedtuple instantiation). This hybrid Python/Go syntax in the input makes the source language ambiguous.

Input field is corrupted: the Python source contains Go artifacts mixed in. Lines 72-76 have `remaining = append(remaining[:best_j], remaining[best_j+1:]...)`, `sc := scs[chosen_i]`, `sc.embedding = nil` — these are Go statements inserted into a Python function body. The input is not valid Python.

Output uses a custom `.Execute()` method that returns `[][]interface{}` (project-specific DuckDB wrapper) and iterates via index-based type assertions (`row[0].(string)`) rather than standard `database/sql` `*sql.Rows.Scan()`. This is a project-specific pattern that departs from idiomatic Go database/sql and may confuse a reviewer unfamiliar with the adapter.

The Python `duckdb.connect()` is called with `read_only` and `config` arguments, but the Go output calls `duckdb.Open(dbPath)` without passing those parameters. They are stored in the struct but never forwarded to the actual connection. Minor behavioral drift in non-critical path (connection config) that a reviewer should confirm.

The Python input only handles all_embeddings, all_chunk_entities, and all_svo_triples views, but the Go output also creates all_domains, all_sources, and all_namespaces views. These extra views are mentioned in the instruction text but have no corresponding Python input code (D4: cited rules/behavior not visible in input). The output goes beyond the provided source (D2: behavioral addition). The view table-name extraction via `strings.TrimPrefix(v.name, "all_")` is also potentially brittle.
